name: E-2
tagline: Start or buy a business in the United States — no green card required
description: The E-2 Treaty Investor Visa allows nationals of treaty countries to enter and work in the United States based on a substantial investment in a U.S. business they own and operate. Unlike EB-5, the E-2 is a non-immigrant visa with no set minimum investment and no job creation quota — making it a faster, more flexible option for entrepreneurs and investors.

requirements: Must be a national of a country that has a qualifying treaty with the United States
Must have invested, or be actively in the process of investing, a 'substantial' amount of capital in a bona fide U.S. enterprise
Investment must be at risk — not passive or idle
Must own at least 50% of the enterprise or have operational control
The enterprise must be more than marginal — capable of generating significantly more income than a living for the investor
Must intend to depart the U.S. when the visa expires (though renewal is available)
benefits: No minimum investment threshold set by law — typically $100,000–$200,000 is considered substantial
No annual cap or lottery — can apply at any time
Initial visa valid up to 5 years, renewable indefinitely in 2-year increments
Spouse receives E-2 dependent visa with full work authorization
Children under 21 may accompany as dependents
Available for both new business startups and acquisitions of existing businesses
Faster processing than EB-5 — often approved within weeks at U.S. consulates

steps.title: Business plan & investment
steps.description: Develop a comprehensive business plan. Make the qualifying investment (or show funds are irrevocably committed).

steps.title: Application preparation
steps.description: Compile the visa application package: DS-160, business plan, investment documentation, source of funds.

steps.title: Consular interview
steps.description: Attend interview at U.S. embassy or consulate in your country. Most E-2 visas are processed at consulates, not through USCIS.

steps.title: Entry & business operation
steps.description: Enter the U.S. and begin operating your business. Maintain E-2 status by continuing to operate the qualifying enterprise.

processing.timeline: 2–8 weeks at most U.S. consulates. Some consulates offer expedited processing.
processing.cost: Visa application fee: $315. No minimum investment set by law — typically $100,000+ is required to demonstrate 'substantial' investment. Attorney fees additional.
documents: Detailed business plan with 5-year financial projections
Proof of investment funds (bank statements, wire transfer records)

faqs.question: Which countries qualify for the E-2 visa?
faqs.answer: The U.S. has E-2 treaties with over 80 countries, including the UK, Germany, France, Japan, South Korea, Australia, Italy, Spain, Turkey, and many others. Notably, India, China, Brazil, and Russia do not have E-2 treaties. If your country is not on the treaty list, EB-5 or other visa categories may be alternatives.

faqs.question: How much do I need to invest for an E-2 visa?
faqs.answer: There is no statutory minimum investment. USCIS and consular officers use a 'proportionality test' — the investment must be substantial relative to the total cost of establishing the enterprise. For a business requiring $500,000 in total capital, an investment of $300,000+ would typically be considered substantial. For lower-cost businesses, a higher percentage of the total is required. In practice, $100,000–$200,000 is often a reasonable starting point.

faqs.question: Can I buy an existing business for an E-2 visa?
faqs.answer: Yes. Purchasing an existing U.S. business is a common E-2 strategy. The business must be a bona fide commercial enterprise (not marginal), and the purchase price must represent a substantial investment. Franchise businesses are also commonly used for E-2 purposes.

faqs.question: Can E-2 lead to a green card?
faqs.answer: The E-2 itself does not lead directly to a green card — it is a non-immigrant visa. However, E-2 investors often pursue parallel green card strategies, such as EB-5 (using the business as the qualifying EB-5 enterprise if it meets the requirements), EB-1C (multinational manager/executive), or sponsoring employees for green cards. We help clients structure their immigration strategy to keep green card options open.

faqs.question: How long can I stay on E-2 status?
faqs.answer: E-2 status is typically granted in 2-year increments when entering the U.S., with the underlying visa valid for up to 5 years (depending on your treaty country). There is no maximum number of renewals — you can remain in E-2 status indefinitely as long as you maintain a qualifying investment and the enterprise remains operational and non-marginal.

faqs.question: Can my spouse work on E-2?
faqs.answer: Yes. E-2 dependent spouses are automatically granted work authorization — they do not need to file a separate EAD application. This is one of the significant advantages of the E-2 visa over many other categories.
